notes.anchor: I shall make use of his ship, and of him as my fellow-passenger
notes.note: With the regular sailings disrupted by war, private travellers depended on whatever vessels their friends could supply. Cicero, planning his crossing to Greece, was glad to secure both a ship and an agreeable companion for the voyage.

notes.anchor: The inflammation of the eyes keeps flaring up afresh
notes.note: Lippitudo, chronic sore or running eyes, was a frequent complaint of Cicero's that he names as the reason his letters are sometimes short or dictated. Here it hampers his own handwriting.

notes.anchor: what holds us up is the equinox, which has been very rough
notes.note: The spring equinox was notorious among ancient sailors for storms, and prudent travellers avoided putting to sea around it. The rough weather of late May 49 BC delayed Cicero's intended crossing.

A diploma was an official travel-permit or pass, here required because Caesar's authorities controlled who could leave Italy. Cicero had assumed Atticus held one because he had drawn passes for his slaves and spoken of setting out.

notes.anchor: The seventeenth day before the Kalends of June
notes.note: Roman dates counted backwards and inclusively from the Kalends (1st of the month); this is 16 May 49 BC. Letters were commonly dated only at the foot, as here.
